bibliographic_data.title: The capabilities and performance of the Automated Planet Finder Telescope with the implementation of a dynamic scheduler
bibliographic_data.abstract: We report initial performance results emerging from 600 hours of observations with the Automated Planet Finder (APF) telescope and Levy Spectrometer located at UCO/Lick Observatory. We have obtained multiple spectra of 80 G, K and M-type stars, which comprise 4,954 individual Doppler radial velocity (RV) measurements with a median internal uncertainty of 1.35 m s−1. We find a strong correlation between the number of photons accumulated in the 5000-6200 Å iodine region of the spectrum and the resulting internal uncertainty estimates. Additionally, we find an offset between the population of G and K stars and M stars within the data set when comparing these parameters. As a consequence of their increased spectral line densities, M-type stars permit the same level of internal uncertainty with 2x fewer photons than G-type and K-type stars. When observing M stars, we show that the APF/Levy has essentially the same speed-on-sky as Keck/HIRES for precision RVs. In the interest of using the APF for long-duration RV surveys, we have designed and implemented a dynamic scheduling algorithm. We discuss the operation of the scheduler, which monitors ambient conditions and combines on-sky information with a database of survey targets to make intelligent, real-time targeting decisions.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper reports initial performance results from the APF telescope and introduces a dynamic scheduling algorithm for long-duration RV surveys.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors obtained multiple spectra of G, K, and M-type stars to measure Doppler radial velocity (RV) with a median internal uncertainty of 1.35 m s−1. They also developed a dynamic scheduler that monitors ambient conditions and combines on-sky information with a database of survey targets for real-time targeting decisions.
outcomes: A strong correlation between the number of photons accumulated in the iodine region and RV measurement uncertainties.
M-type stars permit the same level of internal uncertainty with 2x fewer photons than G-type and K-type stars.
The APF/Levy has essentially the same speed-on-sky as Keck/HIRES for precision RVs when observing M stars.
A dynamic scheduling algorithm was designed to improve efficiency in long-duration RV surveys.
core_content.full_text_markdown: In this paper, we report initial performance results from 600 hours of observations with the Automated Planet Finder (APF) telescope and Levy Spectrometer located at UCO/Lick Observatory. The APF is a high-resolution echelle spectrograph capable of achieving 1 m/s Doppler velocity precision. We obtained multiple spectra of G, K, and M-type stars, resulting in 4,954 individual Doppler radial velocity (RV) measurements with a median internal uncertainty of 1.35 m s−1. The study reveals that there is a strong correlation between the number of photons accumulated in the iodine region of the spectrum and the resulting RV measurement uncertainties. Additionally, M-type stars permit the same level of internal uncertainty with twice as few photons compared to G-type and K-type stars. When observing M stars, the APF/Levy has essentially the same speed-on-sky as Keck/HIRES for precision RVs. To support long-running surveys, we have developed a dynamic scheduling algorithm that monitors ambient conditions and combines on-sky information with a database of survey targets to make intelligent, real-time targeting decisions.
